content.text: Luigi is a Python framework developed by Spotify for building long-running batch data pipelines. It handles the complexity of dependency resolution, task scheduling, failure recovery, and visualizing pipeline progress — all the infrastructure concerns that become painful when orchestrating dozens or hundreds of interdependent data processing steps.
The core philosophy of Luigi is simple:
every unit of work is a Task, every Task produces an Output, and Tasks declare their dependencies on other Tasks.
Luigi figures out the rest — what needs to run, in what order, and what can be skipped because it already completed.
Raw data pipelines built as scripts quickly become brittle. You end up with chains of Python scripts, shell scripts, or cron jobs where:
One failure silently breaks everything downstream
There is no visibility into what ran, what failed, or what's pending
Adding a new step requires manually untangling dependencies
Luigi solves all of these by making dependencies
from the task definitions you write.
— anything that runs periodically and processes large chunks of data in stages.
, you declare targets and their prerequisites.
figures out what's already built (by checking file timestamps) and only rebuilds what's stale or missing. Luigi works identically: each Task declares what it

(a file, a database record, an S3 object). Luigi checks whether the output already exists. If it does, the task is complete. If not, Luigi first ensures all requirements are complete, then runs the task.
idempotency is built into the model
— re-running a pipeline after a partial failure automatically resumes from where it left off, skipping everything already done.
A Task is the fundamental unit of work in Luigi. It is a Python class that inherits from
— returns the upstream Task(s) this Task depends on
representing the expected output of this Task
A Target represents the output of a Task and is Luigi's mechanism for checking whether a Task has already completed. Luigi calls
before deciding to run a Task. If it returns
— a file on the local filesystem
— an object in Google Cloud Storage
— a row in a PostgreSQL table
You can write custom Targets by subclassing
Tasks can be parameterized, making them reusable across different dates, configurations, or input paths. Luigi provides a rich set of parameter types:

class ProcessData(luigi.Task):
    date = luigi.DateParameter()               # datetime.date
    environment = luigi.Parameter(default="prod")  # string
    batch_size = luigi.IntParameter(default=1000)  # integer
    debug = luigi.BoolParameter(default=False)     # boolean
Parameters become part of the Task's identity — two Tasks with the same class but different parameter values are treated as
When Luigi runs a pipeline, it walks the dependency graph defined by

calls recursively and builds a DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph). It then runs Tasks in topological order, starting from leaves (Tasks with no dependencies) and working toward the root (the Task you asked Luigi to run).
have no shared dependencies, they can run

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  Luigi Central Scheduler             │
│   (luigid process — tracks task state, serves UI)   │
└────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────┘
                     │  Worker checks in, gets tasks
          ┌──────────▼──────────┐
          │    Luigi Worker      │  (your Python process)
          │                      │
          │  1. Resolve DAG      │
          │  2. Check outputs    │
          │  3. Run pending      │
          │     tasks            │
          └──────────┬──────────┘
                     │
         ┌───────────┼────────────┐
         ▼           ▼            ▼
    Task A       Task B       Task C
  (reads S3)  (queries DB)  (writes file)
         │           │            │
         └───────────┴────────────┘
                     │
              ┌──────▼──────┐
              │   Target     │
              │  .exists()   │
              │  .open()     │
              └─────────────┘
) is an optional but recommended daemon process that:
Tracks the state of all Tasks across all workers
Prevents duplicate execution when multiple workers are running
Serves the web UI for pipeline visualization
Maintains history of completed and failed tasks
, which is fine for single-process pipelines but doesn't support parallelism or the web UI.
How It Works — Step by Step
Step 1: You invoke Luigi with a target Task.
Step 2: Luigi instantiates the Task and calls
It recursively instantiates all upstream Tasks, building the full DAG.
Tasks whose output already exists are marked
Step 4: Luigi identifies Tasks that are ready to run.
A Task is ready when all its dependencies are COMPLETE but its own output does not yet exist.
Step 5: Luigi dispatches ready Tasks to worker threads.
, up to four Tasks can run simultaneously if they are independent.
On success, the output Target is created. Luigi re-evaluates the DAG — newly unblocked downstream Tasks become runnable.
Step 7: On failure, Luigi marks the Task FAILED.
All downstream Tasks become PENDING (blocked). The pipeline continues running any independent branches that are not blocked. Luigi does
How to Install and Set Up

pip install luigi

# Optional extras
pip install luigi[s3]        # S3 support
pip install luigi[postgres]  # PostgreSQL target
pip install luigi[gcs]       # Google Cloud Storage
Start the central scheduler (for parallel runs and web UI):
This example builds a small ETL pipeline: download raw data → clean it → aggregate it → generate a report.
On first run, all four Tasks execute in order. On a second run, all four outputs already exist, so Luigi completes instantly with no work done. If you delete
When a Task has multiple independent requirements, Luigi can run them in parallel:
WrapperTask — Triggering Multiple Pipelines at Once
is a special base class for Tasks that have no output of their own. They exist purely to group other Tasks:
runs all four pipelines, parallelizing where possible.
Sometimes a Task depends on data produced by a system outside Luigi's control (a file dropped by an external vendor, a table populated by another team's pipeline).
— it simply checks whether the output exists. If it doesn't exist yet,
For databases, APIs, or any non-file output, implement a custom Target:

import luigi
import psycopg2

class PostgresRecordTarget(luigi.Target):
    def __init__(self, host, database, table, record_id):
        self.host = host
        self.database = database
        self.table = table
        self.record_id = record_id

    def exists(self):
        conn = psycopg2.connect(host=self.host, database=self.database)
        cursor = conn.cursor()
        cursor.execute(
            f"SELECT 1 FROM {self.table} WHERE id = %s LIMIT 1",
            (self.record_id,)
        )
        result = cursor.fetchone()
        conn.close()
        return result is not None


class LoadToDatabase(luigi.Task):
    date = luigi.DateParameter()

    def requires(self):
        return CleanData(date=self.date)

    def output(self):
        return PostgresRecordTarget(
            host="localhost",
            database="analytics",
            table="pipeline_log",
            record_id=str(self.date),
        )

    def run(self):
        with self.input().open("r") as f:
            data = f.read()
        # insert data into database
        # then insert a completion record so output().exists() returns True
        conn = psycopg2.connect(host="localhost", database="analytics")
        cursor = conn.cursor()
        cursor.execute(
            "INSERT INTO pipeline_log (id, status) VALUES (%s, %s)",
            (str(self.date), "complete")
        )
        conn.commit()
        conn.close()
file (INI format) in the current directory or from

[core]
default-scheduler-host = localhost
default-scheduler-port = 8082
workers = 8
log_level = INFO

[scheduler]
record_task_history = True
state_path = /var/lib/luigi/state.pickle

[retcode]
unhandled_exception = 4
missing_data = 4
task_failed = 1
already_running = 10
not_run = 25
Task classes can also declare their own configuration sections:
Real-World Example — Daily ML Feature Pipeline
A production scenario at moderate scale: generating ML training features from raw event logs, running nightly for the previous day's data.
This pipeline automatically ensures that 37 days of
are complete before training — and it parallelizes all the independent date partitions simultaneously.
Writing output before the computation succeeds.

crashes halfway through and has already partially written the output file, Luigi will think the Task succeeded on the next run. The fix is to write to a temporary file and atomically rename it at the end, or use Luigi's built-in atomic writes:

# Luigi's LocalTarget.open("w") uses a temporary file automatically
# and only moves it into place when the context manager exits cleanly
with self.output().open("w") as f:
    f.write(result)  # Only committed if no exception is raised
Mutable global state in Task classes.
Luigi may instantiate Tasks multiple times in different contexts. Avoid storing state as instance variables in
is a list. If it returns a dict,
Luigi parameters must be serializable to strings for the scheduler and CLI. Use
Local directories must exist before writing. Call
Luigi does not detect circular dependencies gracefully — it will hang or produce cryptic errors. Design DAGs carefully and test with small pipelines first.
Always include the date (or date+hour) in your output paths. This makes backfilling trivial — just run the pipeline with earlier dates.
handles this. For S3 and custom targets, ensure your
only creates the output after full success — often done with a
Move heavy logic into importable functions or separate modules. This makes Tasks testable in isolation:
Define a base class with a consistent
method to avoid path inconsistencies across Tasks:
Always validate a new pipeline with local targets and sample data before pointing it at production systems.
Luigi's worker parallelism is thread-based within a single Python process. With

, Luigi runs up to N Tasks simultaneously using Python threads. Because most Luigi Tasks are I/O-bound (waiting for Spark jobs, database queries, or file operations), the GIL is not usually a bottleneck.
For very large pipelines with hundreds of Tasks, the central scheduler (
) is essential — it prevents duplicate execution when multiple worker processes run the same pipeline. Without it, two workers could both decide to run the same Task simultaneously.

A single Luigi worker process is not meant to run thousands of simultaneous Tasks. For pipelines that need to schedule hundreds of parallel jobs, the pattern is to have Luigi Tasks submit work to an external cluster (Spark, Kubernetes, AWS Batch) and poll for completion, rather than doing heavy computation inline.

stores pipeline state in a pickle file by default. For high-availability setups, this is a single point of failure. For production, use a proper database backend or consider Airflow/Prefect which have more robust state backends.
is running. Shows the dependency graph, task status (PENDING, RUNNING, DONE, FAILED), and history at
— Built-in integrations for S3, GCS, HDFS, PostgreSQL, MySQL, Redshift, BigQuery, Spark, Hadoop, Kubernetes, and more.
— Test individual Tasks in isolation by calling

def test_clean_data(tmp_path):
    raw = tmp_path / "raw.csv"
    raw.write_text("user_id,amount,category\nu001,49.99,electronics\n")
    
    task = CleanData(date=date(2024, 1, 15))
    # Monkeypatch input/output to use tmp_path files
    task.input = lambda: luigi.LocalTarget(str(raw))
    task.output = lambda: luigi.LocalTarget(str(tmp_path / "clean.csv"))
    task.run()
    
    assert (tmp_path / "clean.csv").exists()
as a daemon managed by systemd or supervisor, with the state file on a persistent volume.
Simple batch ETL, teams already on Python
Luigi's main advantages over Airflow are

— there's no separate webserver, scheduler daemon, or worker daemon to manage. Its main disadvantage is that it lacks Airflow's rich operator ecosystem, triggering rules, SLA monitoring, and mature retry/alerting infrastructure.
Never hardcode credentials in Task parameters or class bodies. Use environment variables,
(with restricted file permissions), or a secrets manager like AWS Secrets Manager or HashiCorp Vault accessed at runtime inside

is safe to re-execute. If a Task writes to a database, it should be an upsert or use a transaction that rolls back cleanly. Partial writes that leave the output Target in an incomplete state cause hard-to-diagnose bugs.
For distributed file formats (Parquet partitions, multi-file outputs), write a sentinel
file as the Luigi Target after all files are written. This ensures Luigi considers the Task complete only when the entire output set is ready, not just the first file.
Luigi marks failed Tasks and their downstream dependents as blocked but does not stop other independent branches. Set up alerting by subclassing

writes to a temporary path and renames atomically on close. This prevents downstream Tasks from reading a partially-written file. For S3, use multipart uploads and only write the sentinel file after the data upload completes.
— the framework derives the execution order automatically from these declarations.
is Luigi's core idempotency mechanism — completed work is never repeated, and pipelines resume automatically after failures.
make Tasks reusable and date-partitioned pipelines trivial to backfill.
enables parallelism, the web UI, and prevents duplicate execution across multiple workers.
batch ETL, feature pipelines, and report generation
where workloads are periodic, checkpointing matters, and operational simplicity is valued over rich scheduling features.

For very large-scale orchestration with complex retry logic, SLAs, and team collaboration features, Airflow or Prefect are more appropriate — but Luigi remains an excellent choice for teams that want a lightweight, pure-Python solution with zero infrastructure overhead.
